title.emphasis: The Legal Framework: California's Retroactive ABC Test

section.para: The analysis of any labor relationship in California begins with the "ABC test," a rigid, three-pronged standard established in the landmark Dynamex decision and applied retroactively.1 This test presumes a worker is an employee unless the hiring entity can prove
all three of the following conditions:

listitem.para: (A) The worker is free from the control and direction of the hiring entity.
(B) The worker performs work that is outside the usual course of the hiring entity's business.
(C) The worker is customarily engaged in an independently established trade, occupation, or business.1

section.para: Before the ABC test is applied, a court must first determine that the relationship involves "labor or services for remuneration".2 In the case of a patron paying for a specific creative work, this threshold is easily met. The creator provides skilled labor, and the patronage is the remuneration. Under California's "Predominant Factor Test," the essence of this specific agreement is the service of creating IP, not merely the sale of a finished good.1
